free_text: Measurement of the relative differential cross
sections of the 1H(n,el) reaction in the neutron
energy range from 6 MeV to 52 MeV
State Key Laboratory of Particle Detection
and Electronics, Beijing
The Back-n white neutron source at
China Spallation Neutron Source (CSNS)
The white neutron beam was produced by a
double bunched proton beam (1.6 GeV, ~50 kW)
bombarding a tungsten target. The interval between
the two proton bunches was 410 ns. The diameter of
the neutron beam was about 15 mm at the sample
position,the neutron flux was about 9.35E105 n/cm2/s.
A square graphite foil for background measurement,
the side length is 76.8 mm, and the thickness is
8.820 mg/cm2. A square low-density polyethylene for
foreground measurement, the side length is 76.8 mm,
and the thickness is 9.476 mg/cm2.
A light-charged particle
detector array system mainly consisted of delta E-E
telescope array and a vacuum chamber. 10 telescopes
were used. The delta E detectors were the Si-PIN
detectors 300 um in thickness, and the active area of
each Si-PIN detector was squared 2.5 cm in side
length, except one detector which was circular 2.0 cm
in diameter. The E detectors were cubic (3.0 cm X 3.0
cm X 3.0 cm) CsI detectors.
The length of neutron flight path was 57.99 m.
The energy-loss (delta E) versus stopping
energy (E) method is used to identify different types
of charged particles.
Total uncertainty
Statistical error of the valid proton events
Background subtraction
Upper limit of the coincidence time
Determination of the area of proton events in
the delta E-E spectrum
Determination of the valid-events-area in
En-(delta E + kCsI X E) and En-E spectra
Detection solid angle of the delta E-E detector
Detection efficiency for recoil protons
Deconvolution of the spread of the detection
angle
Table 4 of Eur.Phys.J.A(2021)57:6
The detailed uncertainties were received
from Haoyu Jiang
Jimin Wang (CNDC)
The relative differential cross sections were
normalized using the relative differential cross
section at about 150 deg scattering angle in the
center-of-mass system.
